coredata.dc:description: In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with remarkable photodynamic antibacterial properties by a hydrothermal method. The composite film was prepared by solvent casting method, compounding N-CDs with chitosan (CS). The morphology and structure of the films were analyzed by Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscope (SEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), and transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques. The films' mechanical, barrier, thermal stability, and antibacterial properties were analyzed. A preservation test of the films was studied on the samples of pork, volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), total viable count (TVC), and pH were determined. Besides, the effect of film on the preservation of blueberries was observed. The study found that, compared with the CS film, the CS/N-CDs composite film is strong and flexible, with good UV light barrier performance. The prepared CS/7 % N-CDs composites showed high photodynamic antibacterial rates of 91.2 % and 99.9 % for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. In the preservation of pork, it was found that its pH, TVB-N, and TVC indicators were significantly lower. The extent of mold contamination and anthocyanin loss was less in the CS/3 % N-CDs composite film-coated group, which could greatly extend the shelf life of food.

head.ce:title: Photodynamic antibacterial chitosan/nitrogen-doped carbon dots composite packaging film for food preservation applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with remarkable photodynamic antibacterial properties by a hydrothermal method. The composite film was prepared by solvent casting method, compounding N-CDs with chitosan (CS). The morphology and structure of the films were analyzed by Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscope (SEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), and transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques. The films' mechanical, barrier, thermal stability, and antibacterial properties were analyzed. A preservation test of the films was studied on the samples of pork, volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), total viable count (TVC), and pH were determined. Besides, the effect of film on the preservation of blueberries was observed. The study found that, compared with the CS film, the CS/N-CDs composite film is strong and flexible, with good UV light barrier performance. The prepared CS/7 % N-CDs composites showed high photodynamic antibacterial rates of 91.2 % and 99.9 % for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. In the preservation of pork, it was found that its pH, TVB-N, and TVC indicators were significantly lower. The extent of mold contamination and anthocyanin loss was less in the CS/3 % N-CDs composite film-coated group, which could greatly extend the shelf life of food.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the composite film.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the popularity of the concept of a healthy diet, the dietary structure has undergone tremendous changes. As more protein and vitamins are needed, the demand for meat and fruit is increasing year by year. Chilled pork is rich in a variety of nutrients that are beneficial to human health. Blueberries contain abundant anthocyanins and antioxidants, which scavenge free radicals and inhibit melanin pigmentation. But at the same time, pork's nutrient richness makes it a good breeding ground for microbial growth (Xiong, Chen, Warner, & Fang, 2020). Blueberries tend to become contaminated with microorganisms. The economic loss caused by food spoilage and waste is as high as tens of billions every year (Li, Liu, et al., 2021). However, food spoilage will not only reduce the commercial value of food but also seriously endanger human health (Brizzolara, Manganaris, Fotopoulos, Watkins, & Tonutti, 2020; Das, Bej, Murmu, & Banerjee, 2022; Wang, Chen, & Sun, 2020). The main influencing factors leading to food spoilage are inappropriate temperature, humidity, and microbial contamination (Sun et al., 2022). Therefore, it is crucial to take appropriate measures to control the factors that influence food spoilage.

The traditional means of keeping fresh usually use cold chains, but it is expensive and ineffective (Andonegi et al., 2020). Other researchers treat food by spraying it with bacteriostatic agents, but the toxic ingredients come into direct contact with the food, producing some toxic chemicals and influencing the taste and luster of the food surface. Nowadays, the more advocated method is to use cling film packaging food, which not only reduces the high cost, toxic effects, and improves the antibacterial performance, but also to a greater extent ensures food safety (Roy et al., 2021).

As people pay more and more attention to environmental protection and food safety (Liu & Lin, 2019), researchers have prepared a new type of cling film with biomass as the main component. These cling films are mainly divided into three categories, protein cling film, lipid cling film, and polysaccharide cling film (Chen, Wu, et al., 2021). The hydrophilicity of the protein leads to the loose structure and instability of the protein cling film (Ovchinnikov et al., 2017). The lipid film is often uneven, has poor tensile strength, and undesirable odor.

Polysaccharide film has the advantages of high strength, good transparency, and stable chemical properties (Mohamed, El-Sakhawy, & El-Sakhawy, 2020). As the only natural alkaline polysaccharide, chitosan (CS) has wide sources and low cost (Saenz-Mendoza et al., 2020). In addition, CS has many excellent properties, such as degradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and film formation. It has been applied in many fields such as food packaging, membrane separation, gas adsorption, drug delivery, and wastewater treatment (Alhwaige, Agag, Ishida, & Qutubuddin, 2013; Ekrayem, Alhwaige, Elhrari, & Amer, 2021; Saheed, Oh, & Suah, 2021; Thakur & Voicu, 2016). According to research, CS has a good effect on food preservation (Dragostin et al., 2016). CS film can block the air, light, and microorganisms in the external environment and delay the spoilage of food, can also act as a good carrier and combine with compounds, essential oils, and nanoparticles to improve the mechanical, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties of the composite film (Zhou et al., 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi-spherical zero-dimensional materials with a size of <10 nm. Due to its unique photostability, high yield, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and high cost-effectiveness, it has been called the “perfect” carbon nanomaterial (Kurian & Paul, 2021). Various wastes such as tea stems and orange peels are used as carbon sources to prepare CDs, which not only reduces environmental pollution but also improves the economic benefits (Ghosh, Sarkar, Devi, Kim, & Kumar, 2021). The research on CDs is mainly focused on wound healing, fluorescence detection, and metal ion adsorption (Xu et al., 2021). Moreover, CDs are considered a multi-antibacterial material, and their antibacterial mechanism theories are including physical damage, oxidative stress, and inhibition of bacterial metabolism (Chen, Cheng, et al., 2021). In some studies, it has been found that doping CDs can significantly improve the performance of CDs (Matsagar, Yang, Dutta, Ok, & Wu, 2021). In particular, N doping can provide amide and amine groups to CDs, thus nitrogen-doping carbon dots (N-CDs) have a higher positive charge and damage the membrane surface, thus achieving a more effective antimicrobial ability (Stankovic et al., 2018). In addition, a few studies have found that CDs have the photodynamic antibacterial ability, which refers to the activation of photosensitizer by visible light, causing a series of biochemical reactions, thereby inactivating microorganisms (Ghate, Zhou, & Yuk, 2019). Compared with traditional antibacterial treatment, photodynamic sterilization has the advantages of good selectivity, high efficiency, and low toxicity. Photoexcited CDs form reactive oxygen free radicals (ROS) through the redox process, which induces inactivation and oxidation of intracellular substances, damage and dysfunction of intracellular organs, and finally bacterial death.

Previously, there has been relatively little research on the use of CDs as raw materials in the preparation of cling film (Liu, Su, Wu, & Shen, 2022). In the limited research of CDs composite cling film, the photodynamic antibacterial effect, and the subsequent practical fresh-keeping test of the film are often lacking.

In this context, the purpose of the study is to combine N-CDs prepared from “waste source” lemon peel with CS, to comprehensively improve its excellent performance. Evaluating the CS matrix with different concentrations N-CDs films' physicochemical, thermal, barrier, mechanical, and biological properties. Applying the films to pork and blueberry. It will be a very meaningful experiment.

Lemon peels were collected from a supermarket waste in Ya'an, China. Chitosan (MW: 100–200 kDa, deacetylation degree: 95 %) and glycerin (99 %) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid, sulfuric acid, boric acid, magnesium oxide, sodium chloride, ethylenediamine, potassium chloride, hydrochloric acid, and ethanol were purchased from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and potato dextrose agar (PDA) were purchased from Qingdao Hope Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China). Tryptic soy agar (TSA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Methyl red, bromocresol green, and C2H3O2Na·3H2O were provided by Shanghai Yuan Ye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). E. coli (ATCC 10536) and S. aureus (ATCC 29213) were employed in this study purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA).

The synthesis of N-CDs was according to the previous method with small modifications (Hu et al., 2021). 3 g of lemon peel powder was dispersed in 50 mL of ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm), stirring at room temperature for 30 min. Adding 2 mL of ethylenediamine to the above solution, continue to stir for 3 h. The hydrothermal reaction is performed on the mixed solution at 180 °C for 8 h and centrifuged the resulting solution (at 8000 rpm for 10 min), collecting the supernatant fluid and filtering with the 0.22 μm cellulose filter. After that, dialyzed for 24 h with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da, and the dialysate was changed every 8 h. The dialysate was the prepared N-CDs.

Nanocomposite films were prepared by solvent casting method using CS and N-CDs as raw materials. CS (3 wt%) was dissolved in 70 mL of acetic acid (1 vol%) and stirred at room temperature for 2 h. Glycerol (1 vol%) was added to the solution and stirred for 2 h. Different concentrations of N-CDs (1, 3, 5, 7 vol%) were added to the solution and sonicated at 25 °C for 2 h to remove the foam. The above solution was poured on a glass plate, and placed in an oven at 40 °C for 6 h to form the film. Peeling the film from the plate, it was stored at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity (RH). The preparation method of pure CS film is the same as above. The pure CS film and the prepared 1, 3, 5, and 7 vol% N-CDs composite film were named CS, CS/1 % N-CDs, CS/3 % N-CDs, CS/5 % N-CDs, and CS/7 % N-CDs, respectively.

The morphology and structure of N-CDs were explored by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (JEOL-2100F, electron microscope JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. All sample fractured cross-section was observed with a scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Thermal Fisher, FEI QUANTA 250, America) (15 kV, 11 mm). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the films were recorded on spectroscopy (Thermo Nicolet, USA), equipped with horizontal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) crystal (ZnSe). The spectrum was recorded at a wave number of 650–4000 cm−1 over 32 scans.

The surface structure of the film was characterized by an atomic force microscope (Bruker Dimension Icon, German), and the roughness of the film was measured. Gwyddion software was used to draw the image, and the film transverse profile was taken to observe its thickness distribution. Parameters such as mean surface roughness (Ra) and root mean square surface roughness (Rq) were calculated by NanoScope Analysis software. The surface morphology of films was evaluated comprehensively.

Film thickness was measured by a micrometer (KEYENCE, CL-3000, Japan) according to a method with slight modifications (Qiu et al., 2021). The results are the average of 10 points that were randomly sampled. Elongation at break (EB), tensile strength (TS), and Young's modulus (E) were measured at 25 °C, using a texture analyzer (TA-XT plus, Stable Micro System Co. Ltd., UK). Specimens for each sample were cut into the size of 80 mm × 15 mm and equilibrated at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 2 days.

The light-barrier properties of films were determined by measuring their light absorption at wavelengths ranging from 200 nm to 800 nm, using a Varioskan Flash multimode reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA). Each film sample was cut off into an equal size of 2.5 cm × 1 cm rectangular piece.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability and contact angle
ce:section.ce:para:
The water vapor permeability (WVP) of the film samples was determined in a controlled humidity environment chamber, according to the previously reported method with slight modifications (Yadav, Mehrotra, & Dutta, 2021). The test was subjected to a temperature of 38 ± 0.5 °C and a relative humidity of 90 ± 2 %. WVP values were calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
WVP =
Δm × d
A × t × Δ P
where WVP represents the measured water vapor permeability (g·m−1·s−1·Pa−1) through a film, ∆ m is the weight change (g), d is the thickness (cm), A is the test area (cm2), t is time (s), and ΔP is the water vapor pressure difference (Pa).

The contact angle (CA) of water in the air on the film surface was performed employing an optical measuring device (OCA20, Germany). A 5 μL droplet of distilled water was placed on the film surface to estimate its hydrophobic character. The measurements for each sample were repeated at least three times and the average value is given.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiments were performed on a SIINT instrument (Q200, TA Instruments, USA) to monitor the thermal stability of each film, the films (≈5 mg) were separately introduced into the aluminum pan, and an empty aluminum pan was used as a reference. The test program was set from 20 °C to 200 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min in an inert gas (nitrogen).

The process of thermogravimetric (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) analysis was done by using a TA analyzer (SDT600, TA Instruments, USA). The test parameters are the heating rate of 10 °C/min, a heating range of 30–800 °C, sample mass of about 5 mg, in nitrogen gas.

The antibacterial activity of film samples against Gram-negative pathogens (E. coli) and Gram-positive pathogens (S. aureus) was evaluated. The pure culture of the bacteria to be tested was streaked on the TSA plate, and after culturing at 37 °C for 18–24 h, 3–5 single colonies were dipped in the inoculation loop and mixed well in 150 mL of LB broth and shaken at 30 °C for 12 h, and determining the concentration of the bacteria (~109 CFU·mL−1). Dispense 5 mL into centrifuge tubes containing 50 mg films, and perform xenon light illumination and dark treatment. Dilute the treated bacterial solution to a certain concentration, take 100 μL and spread it on the TSA plate and cultivate in a constant temperature incubator for 18–24 h. Observe and record the growth of the petri dish. Then the growth inhibition of light and dark contrast was measured according to Eq. (2):
(2)
Growth
inhibition
%
=
N 0
− N
N 0
where No and N are the number of colonies without irradiation and under 8 h irradiation, respectively.

Fresh lean pork was purchased from a supermarket. Divide each 50 g piece of whole pork into an uncovered transparent plastic cup and seal it with film samples. The atmosphere in the cups was air. All sealed pork samples were stored in a transparent glass door upright fridge at 4 °C, and the whole test ensure consistent LED light exposure. Pork samples were measured on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 for mass loss (ML), pH, total volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N), and total viable account (TVC). The cutting, applying, packaging, and handling process of the pork samples were conducted in a sterile environment at room temperature. Each test was repeated three times.

Mass loss percentage of chilled pork during storage is calculated by Eq. (3):
(3)
Mass Loss
%
=
W o
−
W t
W o
where Wo and Wt are the initial weight and final weight (g) of the pork.

The interior pH of pork samples was measured by a pH meter (BPH-7200, BELL Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd., China) according to a method with modifications (Xiong, Li, Warner, & Fang, 2020). The pH meter was calibrated by pH 4.01, 6.86, and 9.18 buffers at 25 °C before use.

The content of volatile base nitrogen (TVB-N) in pork samples was evaluated according to a method reported previously and with some modifications (Zhang et al., 2022). After 10 g of pork packed in a sampling bag were beaten evenly with a homogenizer (Bag Mixer 400CC, Interscience, France), adding 75 mL of distilled water and 1 g of magnesium oxide, and thoroughly mixed. The test was carried out on a Kjeldahl nitrogen analyzer (SKD-100, Shanghai Peiou Analysis Instrument Co., Ltd., China), the receiving solution was 30 mL of boric acid, and the distillation time was 180 s.

The total viable count (TVC) was determined using plate count agar to evaluate the microbial growth in pork samples. About 25 g of pork and 225 mL 0.9 % NaCl water were transferred into a sampling bag and homogenized for 1 min. The liquid mixture was used to make ten-fold serial dilutions with 0.9 % NaCl water, and 100 μL of each dilution was spread on an agar plate and then cultured at 25 °C for 2 days.

The blueberries were stored at room temperature (25 °C) and divided into four groups blank group, conventional membrane PE film group, CS film, and composite film. The shelf life of blueberries was compared by periodic photo processing.

The mold colonies were determined using PDA to evaluate the mold growth in blueberry samples. The blueberry homogenate was used to make ten-fold serial dilutions with 0.9 % NaCl water, and 100 μL of each dilution was spread on an agar plate and then cultured at 30 °C for 2–3 days.

The content of anthocyanin was measured by the pH differential method referring to previous studies with some modifications (Liu, Xie, et al., 2022). The homogenized blueberries (5 g) were added with 50 mL extract solution (hydrochloric acid:80 % ethanol = 3:97) at 5 °C for 30 min ultrasonic extraction. Centrifuge the extract at 8000 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was diluted with the extraction solution, and then added into the buffer solution of potassium chloride (pH = 1) and sodium acetate (pH = 4.5), respectively. The solutions were read at 700 nm and 525 nm by the Varioskan Flash multimode reader.

The TEM images of N-CDs are shown in Fig. 1A and B. The N-CDs are semblable in shape and size as previously reported (Chung, Revia, & Zhang, 2021). The synthesized N-CDs are roughly spherical, with good dispersion. The image shows clear lattice fringes and the lattice gap is 0.23 nm. The average particle size of 100 N-CDs was counted by ImageJ software, as shown in Fig. 1C. The N-CDs had a uniform particle size distribution in the range of 2.34 nm to 5.88 nm with an average diameter of 3.80 ± 0.73 nm.

The pictures of CS film and CS/N-CDs films are shown in Fig. 2 . The prepared films have a good appearance. It can be observed that the surface of the composite films was relatively flat, which can be verified in Fig. 4A. There was no obvious protrusion on the surface of the films in the AFM image. The color of the CS film was colorless, and the color deepened with the increase of the concentration of N-CDs added.

The cross-sectional SEM images of the CS membrane and the CS/3 % N-CDs composite membrane showed similar morphology in Fig. 3 , and the porous structure of the CS membrane was richer. Due to the presence of N-CDs, the density of CS/3 % N-CDs increased. After adding 3 % of N-CDs, the voids in the layered structure of the CS matrix were reduced. This may be the great dispersion of N-CDs in the CS matrix (Hu, Sun, & Wu, 2013).

AFM images are shown in Fig. 4 , 2D topography, cross-section profile, and 3D topography of composite films are shown from left to right. Within the scanning range of 5 μm by 5 μm, it can be intuitively seen from 2D and 3D figures that there is a positive correlation between N-CDs concentration and film roughness. It can also be seen from the midline cross-section profile that the height of the films gradually increases with the increase of N-CDs nanoparticles. In addition, the arithmetic average roughness deviation from the mean line (Ra) and root mean square roughness (Rq) parameters of the film surface were quantitatively analyzed. It can be seen from Fig. 4B that the pure CS film is relatively flat. With the increase of N-CDs concentration, both Ra and Rq gradually increase, and the roughness of the film increases. This corresponds to the previous study, the increase of nanoparticles can cause an agglomeration phenomenon and affect the roughness of the film (Soltanzadeh et al., 2022).

The FTIR spectra of CS/N-CDs films are shown in Fig. 5 . The physical interaction between CS and N-CDs produces a cross-linking effect that affects peak intensity and position. The broad peaks appearing at 3300 cm−1 are mainly due to the hydrogen bonds between N-CDs, CS, and glycerol (Konwar, Gogoi, Majumdar, & Chowdhury, 2015). The peak observed at 2880 cm−1 is attributed to the CH stretching vibration of the alkane groups of CS. The peak at 1550 cm−1 is related to the NH2 bending vibration (Luo et al., 2021). The small peak at 1410 cm−1 is due to the deformation band of CH. The peak at 1150 cm−1 is attributed to the OH bending vibration due to the structure of CS. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 correspond to the characteristic bands of CC and COC, which confirms the existence of sp2 hybridization in N-CDs (Kandra & Bajpai, 2020). In summary, the functional groups of the chitosan-based films did not change characteristically after the addition of N-CDs, proving the good compatibility of CS with N-CDs. The slight changes in the intensity and position of the peaks may be due to the abundant pore structure and functional groups on the surface of CDs.

The thickness values of the composite films are listed in Table 1 . The thickness of CS film and CS/N-CDs composite films were different, and the thickness increased with the increase of N-CDs concentration. The mechanical properties of the tensile strength (TS), elongation at break (EB), and Young's modulus (E) of the films were investigated, where these are the indicators of flexibility and strength. The addition of N-CDs markedly increases the mechanical properties of the films. Compared with pure CS films, the TS and EB of CS/N-CDs composite films increased significantly. Similar results were observed by filling CS with other types of nanofillers, such as clay nanotubes and metal nanoparticles (Cavallaro, Micciulla, Chiappisi, & Lazzara, 2021). The mechanical properties of films are greatly improved by the addition of nanomaterials. When the addition amount was 3 %, the tensile strength and elongation at break showed the highest values. As a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, N-CDs have a high specific surface area and internal disorder. The structure will enhance the cross-linking with CS, enhancing the interaction with the CS matrix (Siddique, Singh, Pramanick, & Ray, 2020). The E of the film is negatively correlated with the EB, indicating that the film is more flexible, which may be due to the excessive addition of N-CDs solution in the membrane matrix, affecting the cross-linking effect in the film (Kalanidhi & Nagaraaj, 2022).

Ultraviolet (UV) light is a powerful oxidant that can disadvantageously affect food products. We recorded the absorbance of the films when exposed to UV radiation from 200 nm to 800 nm. As shown in Fig. 6B, the N-CDs content increased from 0 % to 7 %, and the absorption ability of the film to UV light increased. As the absorbance of the film increased, N-CDs could effectively block UV rays from entering the food. Therefore, CS/N-CDs composite membranes have potential applications in retarding lipid oxidation in photosensitive foods. Moreover, the increase in absorbance can significantly improve the thermal resistance of CS/N-CDs nanocomposite films, which can prolong the shelf life of food. On the other hand, with the increase of the absorbance of the film, the antibacterial ability of the photodynamic therapy of the film will also increase accordingly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability and contact angle
ce:section.ce:para: WVP is one of the important reference factors influencing product quality and shelf life. Possibly due to the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl in N-CDs, these polar components endow N-CDs with excellent water solubility (Li, Li, et al., 2021). The results suggest that the N-CDs aqueous solution may increase the water vapor channels in the microstructure of the film network, thereby reducing the barrier properties of the film against water vapor.

The CA is commonly used as a measure of surface wettability and an indicator of the relative hydrophilicity of the surface. As shown in Fig. 7 , the contact angle of the CS film is 81.5°, and the CA of the CS/N-CDs film gradually decreases with the increase of the N-CDs solution. The results show that the hydrophilicity is enhanced when the N-CDs nanoparticle solution is added (Elsayed et al., 2021).

The DSC profiles of pure CS and composite films are shown in Fig. 8 and Table 2 . DSC analysis is a measurement of the components present in the polymer. The CS film showed the largest peak at around 127 °C, and the endothermic peak of CS was related to the evaporation of bound water and absorbed water. The onset, peak, and end-set temperatures were shifted with the addition of N-CDs slightly. And ΔH of the films increases with the augment of N-CDs. The observed results indicated good thermal stability and biocompatibility between CS and N-CDs.

TG and DTG analysis are important indexes of the thermal stability of films. As can be seen from the DTG curve in Fig. 9a–e, the maximum decomposition temperature of the film is within the temperature range of 280.94–285.37 °C. With the addition of N-CDs, the DTG curve of the film changes slightly, which may be due to the interaction of hydrogen bonds between CS and N-CDs. Three peaks of the DTG curve correspond to the weight loss process of the TG curve. As can be seen in Fig. 9f, the weight loss of the composite films is mainly divided into three stages, namely, the evaporation of water at 60–130 °C, the degradation of glycerol at 160–260 °C, and the decomposition of CS at 270–350 °C (Zhou et al., 2021).

Fig. 10 shows the photocatalytic antibacterial effect of the composite films on Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus. It can be found that CS has antibacterial activity against bacteria. And has a better antibacterial effect on S. aureus than E. coli, which is caused by the difference in cell wall composition between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria is composed of hydrophilic lipopolysaccharide, which can effectively avoid the entry of hydrophobic substances such as CS (Rashki et al., 2021). With the increase of N-CDs concentration, the antibacterial property of the composite film was enhanced. This is due to the N-CDs being zero-dimensional nanomaterial, which high specific surface will increase its contact with the bacteria' cell wall, thereby causing certain mechanical damage to the bacteria. In addition, by N-doping on the CDs, the amide groups and amino groups of CDs are endowed, so that the surface of N-CDs is positively charged, which combines with the negative potential on the surface of bacteria and destroys the surface point of bacteria, resulting in severe damage to the bacterial cell membrane, exudation of cell contents, and ultimately bacterial death (Wu, Li, van der Mei, Busscher, & Ren, 2021). Comparing the irradiated (6 h) and unirradiated (0 h) groups in Fig. 11 , it can be confirmed that the antibacterial activity of bacteria is significantly increased after photocatalysis, and the antibacterial activity of CS/7 % N-CDs is as high as 99.9 % compared to before irradiation. It may be due to the fact that N-CDs act as photosensitizers and are activated by visible light through photodynamic therapy (PDT) to form reactive oxygen radicals (ROS), such as hydroxyl radicals, superoxide, and hydrogen peroxide (Songca & Adjei, 2022). ROS induces inactivation and oxidation of intracellular substances, damage and dysfunction of internal organs, and ultimately bacterial death.

From the chart of chilled pork stored at 4 °C for 10 days in Fig. 12 , it can be seen that with the extension of storage time, the pork wrapped by CS/3 % N-CDs composite film is more shrink, which corresponded to the poor barrier of the film. However, the pork coated with composite film showed the slowest discoloration and deterioration. It can be seen that the PE film and CS film packed pork for 10 days permeated a lot of metamorphic juice. Compared with PE film, the weight loss rate of pork treated with CS film and CS/3 % N-CDs composite film is higher, which is due to the porous structure of the films (Fig. 13A). The porous structure will promote the volatilization of water vapor, which leads to the rapid loss of water in meat products.

pH value is an important indicator for evaluating the freshness of pork. It can be seen from Fig. 13B that the pH value of the CS/3%N-CDs composite film group was much lower. The first drop in pH is due to anaerobic glycolysis of muscle glycogen in pork, which produces lactic acid and adenosine triphosphate (ATP), resulting in a decrease in pH (Odeyemi, Alegbeleye, Strateva, & Stratev, 2020). As the pork is stored longer, the protein generates alkaline nitrogen-containing compounds such as ammonia and amines under the action of bacteria and enzymes, so that the pH value will rise again. The pH of pork treated with CS/3 % N-CDs composite membrane increased slowly, which may be due to the low water vapor barrier property of CS/3 % N-CDs making the hard surface of pork, which inhibited the internal chemical reaction of pork to a certain extent.

The changes in TVB-N in pork samples of different film-packed groups with storage time were shown in Fig. 13C, and the TVB-N content in pork samples increased with the prolongation of storage time. The TVB-N content in the PE film packaging group was significantly higher than that in the CS film and CS/3 % N-CDs composite film packaging groups. The pork packed with CS/3 % N-CDs composite film did not reach the spoilage threshold (15 mg/100 g) until the 10th day, which equals to long shelf life. The increase in TVB-N value was due to the joint action of spoilage microorganisms and enzymes. The growth and reproduction of microorganisms destroyed the cell structure in the meat, so a large number of endogenous enzymes were released from the cells, thereby accelerating the degradation of proteins and the catabolism of amino acids, resulting in the accumulation of volatile bases, resulting in pork odor (Zhang et al., 2019).

The rapid growth of spoilage bacteria and pathogenic bacteria usually accelerates meat spoilage (Mohammadalinejhad, Almasi, & Moradi, 2020). When TVC exceeds 6.00 lg CFU/g, it indicates that the pork has spoiled. During storage, the TVC values of pork samples from all film-packed groups increased rapidly with time (Fig. 13D). In contrast with PE film-packed pork, the TVC of pork packed with the CS film and CS/3 % N-CDs composite film changed slowly. This is mainly due to the good antibacterial activity of CS film and CS/3 % N-CDs film.

In addition, to demonstrate that the prepared composite film can also be applied to fruits, the storage experiment of blueberries was also confirmed. The PE group showed macroscopic mold contamination on the 9th day obviously (Fig. 14 ), which corresponds to Fig. 15A. Compared to other groups, the mold contamination degree of blueberry coated with CS/3 % N-CDs composite membrane was lower. Because the composite films have great antibacterial properties, which effectively inhibit the growth of microorganisms and extend the shelf life of blueberries.

Anthocyanin is an essential functional component in the epidermis of blueberries, which is easily affected by temperature and light (Lacombe et al., 2015). During the storage of blueberries, there will be a process of anthocyanin accumulation, originally (Fig. 15B). The anthocyanin accumulation of blueberry coated with the composite film was faster. With the extension of time, the content of anthocyanin gradually decreases. Moreover, anthocyanin levels of CS/3 % N-CDs composite film packaged blueberries are decreased at a lower rate than other groups. This may be due to the good light-blocking property of the composite film, which effectively prevents the effect of light on anthocyanins. Compared with other groups, the CS/3 % N-CDs composite film demonstrated good storage performance, generally.

In conclusion, The N-CDs were prepared using “green waste” lemon peel as a precursor and combined with CS to develop CS/N-CDs composite membranes with good functional properties. TEM analysis revealed that our synthesized N-CDs were quasi-spherical with uniform size distribution (3.80 nm). SEM and FTIR tests proved that CS and N-CDs had good dispersion, the internal structure of the composite film was regular, and the surface was smooth. The CS/N-CDs composite films showed remarkable mechanical properties, UV-blocking properties, and thermal stability. Compared with the CS film, the absorbance of CS/3 % N-CDs in the UV wavelength range of 200–800 nm was significantly increased. Compared with the CS membrane, the TS and EB of the CS/3 % N-CDs composite membrane were significantly increased to 21.6 MPa and 110.3 %. Due to the photodynamic bacteriostatic activity of N-CDs, the CS/N-CDs composite membrane showed strong antibacterial ability against E. coli (G−) and S. aureus (G+). In addition, CS/N-CDs composite film was found in the pork preservation test, CS/3 % N-CDs composite film can effectively prolong the shelf life of pork, and effectively reduce pH, TVB-N, and TVC. In the storage comparison of blueberries, it was found that the CS/3 % N-CDs composite film stabilized the anthocyanin content and delayed the spoilage of blueberries. Therefore, we conclude that CS/N-CDs can be used as a favorable packaging material to be widely used in food preservation.

Shujuan Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Qiuyan Zeng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xinyu Tan: Data curation. Mengyi Ye: Writing – review & editing. Yanan Zhang: Formal analysis. Likou Zou: Validation. Shuliang Liu: Supervision. Yong Yang: Project administration. Aiping Liu: Visualization. Li He: Resources. Kaidi Hu: Software.
